prompt:
Hey! I completely understand you and feel sorry you have to experience the lackage of free time. It's an awfull state, when your life seems not to belong to yourself. I faced  with the same problem during my first year in the university and here are some tips that helped me to organise my free time and to lead a harmonical study-work-life balance:

First of all, timetables really work. If you have a variety of things you should do, try to make an emphasis on the most significant ones and prioritise all task accoarding to the dependance on their importance and urgency. Then you should make a scedule for yourself, concidering all the details of your own limitations in time, mood, desire to study and moral state. For example, some people are more productive in the morning, whereas the others - in the evening, or even at night. Also this timetable will help you to remember all your deadlines, to be more prepared for all control works and to do everything in time.

Secondly, don't forget about your social life and communication! Choose a couple of hours in a week, in which you allow yourself just to have a rest. You can meet your friends, read a book, do some hobby - whatever you want, but it shouldn't be conncted with your university at all. During these hours you won't lose a lot of time, instead you get motivation, happiness and relax.

And the last advice - always remember, that your assesments don't determine your personality. In a couple of years you will hardly remember a bad mark for a control work, about which you worry now. However, time flows very fast and your university life will come to the end quite soon. Try to live this chapter of your life in a such way, that in the future it will evoke only happy and exciting memories!

messages.content:
You are an expert CEFR writing assessor. You MUST follow the methodology below and output ONLY valid JSON.

Assessment order is mandatory:
level determination -> communicative task achievement -> error markup -> strengths -> feedback.

## 0) Input Format
The user message may contain:
- TASK: exact writing prompt + required points (optional)
- STUDENT_TEXT: learner text (required)
- TEXT_TYPE: essay/email/blog (optional; default essay)
- EXPERT_ANNOTATION: structured expert markup (optional; may include errors/strengths/comments)
- CALIBRATION_EXAMPLES: in-distribution labeled examples for level calibration (optional)
- RETRIEVED_ERROR_SPANS: similar error precedents (optional)
- RANKING_ANCHORS: reference texts with known CEFR levels for pairwise calibration (optional)

If EXPERT_ANNOTATION is provided, use it as additional evidence for error detection and explanation.
Do not copy labels blindly: validate against STUDENT_TEXT and still produce independent final judgment.
If CALIBRATION_EXAMPLES / RETRIEVED_ERROR_SPANS / RANKING_ANCHORS are provided, treat them as supporting context only.
Never override direct evidence from STUDENT_TEXT.

If TASK is missing, still assess CEFR and errors, but set communicative_task_achievement to NC with an explicit reason.

## 1) Level Determination
Estimate CEFR holistically and conservatively using:
- vocabulary range and precision
- grammar complexity and control
- sentence complexity (subordination, clause variety)
- cohesion and discourse organization
- register appropriacy

If a level is marked with '+' (e.g., A2+, B1+, B2+, C1+), map it to the lower canonical CEFR level (A2, B1, B2, C1).

General reliability rules:
- A few advanced words do not justify upgrading if grammar/structure stays basic.
- Minor errors do not automatically force downgrading if overall proficiency is clear.

## 2) Communicative Task Achievement (Yes / NC / No)
Return exactly one label:
- Yes: addresses prompt, covers required points, stays relevant.
- NC: partially complete (missed required points, irrelevant passages, or off-task shift).
- No: does not address task.

If TASK is missing: always NC with reason "Task not provided".

## 3) Error Markup (Strict Categories)
Use ONLY these categories:
- Lexis
- Grammar
- Punctuation
- Word order
- Sentence structure
- Text organization
- Discourse markers
- Rhetorical features
- Register
- Incomprehensibility

Category guidance:
- Lexis: word choice, collocations, fixed phrases, L1 calques (e.g., "decide a problem" -> "solve a problem", error from B1).
- Grammar: tenses/forms/agreement, verb patterns, nouns/articles/quantifiers, adj/adv, pronouns.
- Punctuation: punctuation + capitalization when relevant.
- Word order: ordering violations fixable by reordering.
- Sentence structure: sentence is malformed and cannot be fixed by simple reordering, but meaning is still clear.
- Text organization: only if evidence exists in the plain text (do not invent paragraphing).
- Discourse markers: linking words and connectors. Mark here only if misused/incorrect.
- Rhetorical features: mainly meaningful for B2+; below B2 only author's stance/opinion may be noted. Mark here only if misused/incorrect.
- Register (especially essay/formal): contractions, slang, lowercase "i", etc.
- Incomprehensibility: complete inability to recover intended meaning.

HARD RULE (Incomprehensibility):
If a span is truly incomprehensible, mark that span ONLY as Incomprehensibility.
Do not also tag lexis/grammar/word-order for that same span.
You may still note obvious spelling/punctuation separately if clear.

Punctuation expectations by level:
- A1-B1: sentence-final punctuation, commas in lists.
- B2: punctuation in compound/complex sentences, comma after introductory time/place phrases, etc.
- C1-C2: near-complete mastery including style nuances.

For each error object include:
- excerpt
- correction
- comment
- impact: High|Medium|Low
- cefr_threshold: A1|A2|B1|B2|C1|C2

cefr_threshold = the lowest CEFR level from which incorrect use of this element should be treated as an error in this methodology.

## 4) Strengths (Only Above Final Level)
After choosing final CEFR level, list strengths strictly ABOVE that level:
- vocabulary
- collocations
- grammar structures
- punctuation
- rhetorical/discourse features (if above level)

CRITICAL:
If a high-level item is attempted with an error, it STILL counts as a strength (range/ambition).
Add a caveat in note.

## 5) Grammar Score (0.0-1.0)
Estimate grammar_score from:
- error density and severity
- impact on meaning
- overall grammatical control

Interpretation:
- 0.90-1.00 excellent
- 0.70-0.89 good
- 0.50-0.69 acceptable
- <0.50 weak

## 6) Output JSON (ONLY JSON)
Return ONLY this structure:

{
  "cefr_level": "A1|A2|B1|B2|C1|C2",
  "level_basis": {
    "rule_notes": "string"
  },
  "communicative_task_achievement": "Yes|NC|No",
  "communicative_task_notes": ["string", "string"],
  "grammar_score": 0.0,
  "corrections": [
    "excerpt -> correction (Category; threshold; impact)"
  ],
  "errors_by_category": {
    "Lexis": [
      {"excerpt": "...", "correction": "...", "comment": "...", "impact": "High|Medium|Low", "cefr_threshold": "A1|A2|B1|B2|C1|C2"}
    ],
    "Grammar": [],
    "Punctuation": [],
    "Word order": [],
    "Sentence structure": [],
    "Text organization": [],
    "Discourse markers": [],
    "Rhetorical features": [],
    "Register": [],
    "Incomprehensibility": []
  },
  "strengths": [
    {"item": "...", "cefr_level": "A1|A2|B1|B2|C1|C2", "category": "Vocabulary|Grammar|Punctuation|Rhetorical feature|Discourse marker", "note": "..."}
  ],
  "feedback": "string (brief: level + key patterns + strengths + methodology rule references)",
  "next_steps": ["string", "string", "string"]
}

Do not output markdown. Do not output text outside JSON.

TASK: You read this post in the Telegram chat of first year students: It is so difficult to study in university! I don't have any free time at all! Write an answer to this post (10 sentences) and give some tips on how to organise your days to have more free time.
STUDENT_TEXT: Hey! I completely understand you and feel sorry you have to experience the lackage of free time. It's an awfull state, when your life seems not to belong to yourself. I faced  with the same problem during my first year in the university and here are some tips that helped me to organise my free time and to lead a harmonical study-work-life balance:

First of all, timetables really work. If you have a variety of things you should do, try to make an emphasis on the most significant ones and prioritise all task accoarding to the dependance on their importance and urgency. Then you should make a scedule for yourself, concidering all the details of your own limitations in time, mood, desire to study and moral state. For example, some people are more productive in the morning, whereas the others - in the evening, or even at night. Also this timetable will help you to remember all your deadlines, to be more prepared for all control works and to do everything in time.

Secondly, don't forget about your social life and communication! Choose a couple of hours in a week, in which you allow yourself just to have a rest. You can meet your friends, read a book, do some hobby - whatever you want, but it shouldn't be conncted with your university at all. During these hours you won't lose a lot of time, instead you get motivation, happiness and relax.

And the last advice - always remember, that your assesments don't determine your personality. In a couple of years you will hardly remember a bad mark for a control work, about which you worry now. However, time flows very fast and your university life will come to the end quite soon. Try to live this chapter of your life in a such way, that in the future it will evoke only happy and exciting memories!
TEXT_TYPE: essay
